title: Universal theory of domain-wall width in multi-sublattice Heisenberg magnets
authors: José M. Lendínez, Marta Yanguas, Theodor Griepe, Michael Saur, Rubén M. Otxoa, Levente Rózsa, Unai Atxitia
abstract: We propose a universal expression for the domain-wall width in generic multi-sublattice Heisenberg magnets, applicable to ferro-, antiferro-, and ferrimagnetic orders. The result follows from an exact connection between the domain-wall profile and the long-wavelength spin-wave dispersion, yielding a unified framework for describing magnetic textures across distinct ordering types. The predictions show excellent quantitative agreement with large-scale atomistic spin dynamics simulations over a broad range of exchange and anisotropy values and spin multi-sublattice structures, including three-dimensional rock-salt-type magnets and two-dimensional honeycomb and kagome ferromagnets. Moreover, we establish a general microscopic foundation for the temperature dependence of the domain-wall width. Our approach offers a powerful tool for understanding domain-wall profiles in complex spin systems.
summary: This work develops a universal theoretical framework to calculate the domain-wall width in complex multi-sublattice magnets, unifying descriptions for ferro-, antiferro-, and ferrimagnetic orders. The key method links the DW width directly to the parameters of the long-wavelength spin-wave dispersion. The results provide quantitative agreement with atomistic simulations, offering a powerful tool for understanding magnetic textures across diverse spin systems.
main_problem: The paper aims to derive a universal expression for the domain-wall (DW) width in complex multi-sublattice Heisenberg magnets, applicable across different magnetic ordering types.
main_result: A universal relation, $\delta_{DW} = \sqrt{A_{ eta}/\epsilon_{ eta}^0}$, is proposed that successfully unifies the description of DW width across ferromagnetic, antiferromagnetic, and ferrimagnetic systems.
method: The derivation relies on an exact theoretical connection between the DW profile and the long-wavelength spin-wave (SW) dispersion relation.
model_or_system: The study focuses on multi-sublattice Heisenberg magnets, modeled using Hamiltonians incorporating Heisenberg exchange and uniaxial anisotropy, tested on lattices like rock-salt, honeycomb, and kagome.
key_observables: Domain-wall width ($\delta_{DW}$), spin-wave spectrum parameters (gaps $\epsilon_{\pm}^0$ and stiffnesses $A_{\pm}$), and the temperature dependence of $\delta_{DW}$.
important_parameters: Exchange coupling constants ($J$), anisotropy constants ($D$), temperature ($T$), and the ratio $T/T_c$.
main_assumptions: The approach assumes the validity of connecting the DW profile to the long-wavelength spin-wave theory, and it analyzes the system up to the magnetic ordering transition ($T_c$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the SW spectrum for various magnets, show the calculated DW width versus temperature, and display magnetization profiles confirming the theoretical predictions.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem by noting the failure of simple models, introduces the universal relation derived from SW theory, applies it to various lattice types (FM, AFM, FI), and validates the results against atomistic spin dynamics simulations, including temperature dependence.
why_interesting: While focused on classical magnetism, the connection between macroscopic domain structure (DW width) and microscopic excitations (SW spectrum) is a fundamental problem in many-body dynamics, relevant to understanding thermal fluctuations in quantum magnetic materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic representation of the spin-wave spec- trum of a two-sublattice ferrimagnet, characterized by en- ergy modes ε± q with gaps ε± 0 and spin-wave stiffnesses A± ∝ ∂2 qε± q q=0. (b) Sketch of a domain-wall of width δDW in a two- sublattice ferrimagnet. The orange line indicates the transver- sal component of the sublattice magnetization.

FIG. 2. (a) Sketch of the rock-salt–type magnet used in the atomistic spin model, illustrating ferrimagnetic (FI) ordering. (b) Spin-wave spectrum of the FI system near Γ along the ΓX direction (ΓX = π/a0) at selected reduced temperatures T/Tc. (c) Reduced sublattice magnetization mz r profiles at T/Tc. (d) DW width as a function of T/Tc. Symbols corre- spond to ASD simulations in panels (b-d). Solid lines indicate fits to the SW dispersion in (b) and the universal-relation val- ues from Eq. (1) in (c,d). The green-shaded area in (d) marks the region above the compensation temperature of the FI sys- tem.

FIG. 3. Face-centered cubic ferromagnet: (a) Spin-wave spec- trum near Γ along ΓX direction at selected T/Tc. (b) DW width as a function of T/Tc. Simple cubic antiferromagnet: (c) Spin-wave spectrum near Γ along ΓX direction at selected T/Tc (b) DW width as a function of T/Tc. Symbols corre- spond to ASD simulations in all panels. Solid lines in (a,c) and in (b,d) indicate fits to the SW dispersion and values from the universal relation in Eq. (1), respectively. Panels (b, d) include a sketch of the DW profile for a slice of the system.

FIG. 4. (a) Sketch of a DW profile in a 2D honeycomb ferromagnet. (b) Spin-wave spectrum along the indicated high-symmetry path at zero temperature for selected values of J2. (c) DW width as a function of J2. Solid lines denote LSWT and analytical results for panels (b) and (c), respec- tively. Symbols correspond to ASD simulations; open symbols in (c) correspond to the selected cases shown in (b).

FIG. 5. (a) Sketch of a DW profile in a 2D kagome ferro- magnet. (b) Spin-wave spectrum along the indicated high- symmetry path at zero temperature for selected values of the uniaxial anisotropy D. Solid lines denote LSWT results and symbols serve as a visual guide. (c) δDW and δ− DW contribu- tion as a function of D. Solid lines correspond to analyti- cal results and symbols correspond to ASD simulations, open symbols correspond to the selected cases shown in (b). The inset shows a zoom of the region with high anisotropies.
